#### ✅ Benefits of `make_unique`:
- Prevents memory leaks on exception during object construction
- Shorter and cleaner syntax
- No need to manually use `new`

> Note: `make_unique` is available since **C++14**. In C++11, you have to use raw `new`.

---

#### 🔹 Useful with `decltype(auto)` (C++14) — but even in C++11:
#### ⚠️ Tricky Cases:

---

### 🧠 Internal Quiz on `auto` and `decltype`

---

**Q1.** What is the type of each variable?

> 💡 Think carefully about reference and const behavior!

---

**Q2.** What is the output?

const int x = 42;
auto a = x;               // ✅ int ✔️ (top-level const is dropped)
decltype(x) b = x;        // ✅ const int ✔️ (declared type)
decltype((x)) c = x;      // ✅ const int& ✔️ ((x) is an lvalue)

int arr[] = {1, 2, 3};
for (int x : arr) { ... }

std::map<int, std::string> m = {{1, "A"}, {2, "B"}};
for (const auto& [key, val] : m) { ... }
f(nullptr); // unambiguously calls f(char*)

code_examples.code:
auto add = [](auto a, auto b) { return a + b; };
std::cout << add(1, 2) << ", " << add(1.5, 3.5);
auto add = [](auto a, auto b) { return a + b; };

auto ptr = std::make_unique<int>(42);
auto f = [p = std::move(ptr)] { std::cout << *p; };

int x = 5;
decltype(auto) f() { return (x); }  // Returns int&

auto            → value (copy)
decltype(auto)  → exact (ref, const, etc.)
constexpr int square(int x) { return x * x; }  // Only one statement allowed
auto p = std::make_unique<int>(42);  // No need to specify <int>
auto p = std::unique_ptr<int>(new int(42));  // verbose & unsafe

int a = 10;
int old = std::exchange(a, 20); // old == 10, a == 20

std::tuple<int, double, char> t{1, 2.3, 'a'};
char c = std::get<char>(t);
std::enable_if_t<...> instead of typename std::enable_if<...>::type
